items.question:
You are asked to see a 45-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus for routine follow-up. He feels well and has no acute concerns. You have been encouraging his efforts at lifestyle changes with the hope of achieving some weight loss. His weight of 244 lb (110.9 kg) has been stable over the past year. He is otherwise healthy. He comments that he was relieved when he recently heard on the news that lifestyle changes do not really help people with type 2 diabetes, and he asks whether he must continue his activity program of walking 30 minutes to 5 times per week.

He currently takes metformin, 2000 mg daily; simvastatin, 20 mg daily; and lisinopril, 20 mg daily.

On physical examination, he appears well. Height is 70 in (177.8 cm), and weight is 244 lb (110.9 kg) (BMI = 35 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Blood pressure is 138/80 mm Hg.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc} = 6.8\% $ (0.068)

Serum creatinine = 1.0 mg/dL (88.4 μmol/L)

In this patient, intensive lifestyle changes (goals of weight loss of >7% and 175 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week) would be expected to decrease which of the following?
options: Risk of developing diabetic microvascular disease

Need for insulin therapy A 65-year-old man with a 30-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus returns to see you for a routine follow-up appointment. He was referred to you for management 2 years ago after recurrent episodes of hypoglycemia, some of which had been severe and required the assistance of others. At that time, his insulin therapy was changed to a regimen including once-daily dosing of insulin glargine and multiple daily doses of insulin aspart with meals. The patient keeps a careful blood glucose log in which he records 8 to 10 fingerstick blood glucose readings daily. His glycemic control stabilized following the change in his insulin regimen. Although he periodically experiences blood glucose levels between 60 and 70 mg/dL (3.33-3.89 mmol/L) in the very early morning, his ability to sense hypoglycemia has improved. The patient's most recent episode of severe hypoglycemia occurred 18 months ago during the nighttime while he was asleep. His diabetes is complicated by microalbuminuria, peripheral neuropathy, and retinopathy that has required photocoagulation but not resulted in clinically significant vision impairment. Although he has never experienced a driving mishap related to hypoglycemia, he voluntarily stopped driving when he was referred to you for care. He now asks whether it would be advisable for him to resume operation of a motor vehicle for personal use.

items.question:
A 63-year-old man has recently moved to your city and presents to establish care. He has a history of hypertension and had a myocardial infarction 2 years ago. He has no angina or shortness of breath. He is moderately active and walks daily. He does not smoke cigarettes. Current medications include aspirin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. You note that he is not on a statin, but he reports that he previously tried simvastatin, atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and ezetimibe + simvastatin, but had intolerable myalgias while taking each of these.

On physical examination, pulse rate is 84 beats/min and blood pressure is 128/84 mm Hg. Height is 66 in (167.6 cm), and weight is 220 lb (100 kg) (BMI = 35.5 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Physical examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results (fasting):

Glucose = 115 mg/dL (6.38 mmol/L)

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc}=6.3\% $ (0.063)

HDL cholesterol = 28 mg/dL (0.73 mmol/L)

Total cholesterol = 138 mg/dL (3.57 mmol/L)

LDL cholesterol = 75 mg/dL (1.94 mmol/L)

Triglycerides = 280 mg/dL (3.16 mmol/L)

In addition to recommending a calorie-restricted diet, which of the following should you prescribe?
items.explanation:
## 41 ANSWER: B) Fenofibrate

Despite his low LDL-cholesterol level, this man has already experienced a myocardial infarction and is at very high risk for further cardiovascular disease events. He does not smoke cigarettes, his blood pressure is controlled, and he is taking appropriate cardiovascular medications (aspirin,  $ \beta $-adrenergic blocker, ACE inhibitor). Thus, the only other modifiable cardiovascular risk factor is his dyslipidemia. Most guidelines recommend statins in patients with established cardiovascular disease to further reduce risk; the Adult Treatment Panel III (ATP

III) guidelines recommend treatment to achieve an LDL-cholesterol concentration less than 100 mg/dL (<2.59 mmol/L) with an optional goal of less than 70 mg/dL (<1.81 mmol/L). The newer American Heart Association American College of Cardiology guidelines have moved away from target-driven therapy, and instead recom割high-intensity statin therapy to further reduce risk in patients at high risk such as this one, regardless of LDL-cholesterol level. This recommendation is based in part on studies such as Treat to New Targets (TNT), which compared high-dosage vs low-dosage atorvastatin and found further reductions in events in participants with low LDL-cholesterol levels. However, this patient has had intolerable myalgias with at least 3 statins. Although he not tried pravastatin (Answer A), the fact that he had myalgias with atorvastatin, simvastatin, and rosuvastatin, which differ in their relative hydrophilicity, hepatic metabolism, clearance, and elimination half-life, suggests the will most likely not tolerate pravastatin either. The prevalence of muscle symptoms in statin users is reported to be 5% to 10%; however, the placebo groups generally have a similar prevalence of muscle symptoms, which reflects the high population prevalence of muscle symptoms. In general, if a patient has myalgias to one statin, trying another statin with different pharmacokinetic profiles is appropriate. However, if several have been tried they each cause the same reaction, a tolerable statin is unlikely to be found. A growing body of literature address intermittent statin dosing (such as alternate day) to increase statin tolerability; however, this has not yet been tested with respect to cardiovascular outcomes.

Although statins have the best evidence to support their reduction of cardiovascular disease events, other lip lowering agents can also improve outcomes. Ezetimibe (Answer D) is a cholesterol absorption inhibitor that low LDL cholesterol via a different mechanism than statins. Although this patient previously reported myalgias when taking a statin + ezetimibe combination pill, the symptoms were almost certainly due to the statin component. It would most likely tolerate ezetimibe as monotherapy. However, to date, ezetimibe has not been shown to decrease risk of cardiovascular events. Given that this patient's main lipid abnormalities are low HDL cholesterol and triglycerides, and his LDL-cholesterol level is already fairly low, further LDL lowering with an agent that has not yet been shown to improve outcomes is not the best choice.

Fish oil (Answer C) has been used for its triglyceride-lowering effects, but it is usually recommended for severe hypertriglyceridemia—when triglyceride concentrations are greater than 500 mg/dL (>5.65 mmol/L) and there is a risk of pancreatitis. Fish oil has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular events.

Fibrates (Answer B) have been studied in a variety of monotherapy trials for both primary and secondary prevention. Although fibrates have minimal effects on LDL cholesterol, they can lower triglycerides by up to 50% and raise HDL-cholesterol levels by 10% to 15%. In several studies, including the VA-HIT trial, fibrates reduced cardiovascular events. Similarly, niacin (Answer E), which can lower triglycerides and LDL cholesterol and raise HDL cholesterol, reduced recurrent cardiovascular events and mortality in the Coronar
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 41: "Select lipid-lowering therapy in a statin-intolerant patient."

items.title: Why answer B is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 41: "Select lipid-lowering therapy in a statin-intolerant patient."

items.question:
A 64-year-old woman (a current patient) calls your office with concerns about her medications. She recently started a new diet that is heavy in fruits, including a daily glass of grapefruit for breakfast every day. She recalls the pharmacist telling her that she cannot take statins if she eats grapefruit. She had a myocardial infarction followed by coronary artery bypass grafting 3 years ago and she has since been taking atorvastatin, 40 mg daily. She has hypertension controlled with metoprolol. She is obese but is otherwise healthy. At her last visit, weight was 186 lb (84.5 kg) and height was 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 31.9 kg/m $ ^{2} $).

Recent laboratory test results (fasting) while on atorvastatin (but before starting the grapefruit diet):

Total cholesterol = 142 mg/dL (3.68 mmol/L)

Triglycerides = 98 mg/dL (1.11 mmol/L)

HDL cholesterol = 42 mg/dL (1.09 mmol/L)

Creatinine = 0.7 mg/dL (61.9 μmol/L)

LDL cholesterol = 69 mg/dL (1.79 mmol/L)

Alanine aminotransferase = 28 U/L (0.47 μkat/L)

Which of the following should be your advice?
options: Stop the statin and start a fibrate
Stop the statin and start ezetimibe
Continue the current statin at the same dosage
items.explanation:
## 52 ANSWER: E) Continue the current statin at the same dosage 52 The issue in this vignette is the safety of statin use in patients who consume grapefruit (fruit or juice). The lay literature is ripe with danger warnings about the consumption of grapefruit while taking statins, and patients are routinely counseled to avoid grapefruit or its juice when taking certain medications, including statins. Grapefruit and its juice contain a number of compounds that irreversibly inhibit CYP3A enzymes that are key in the metabolism of a number of drugs. Most statins undergo significant metabolism by the CYP3A system; thus, if the enzymes are inhibited, this leads to increased systemic plasma concentrations of statins, which has the potential to increase clinical and adverse effects. Because the CYP3A system is irreversibly inhibited, this can have effects on drug clearance for the several days after even a single dose. However, the impact of inhibiting CYP3A on statin metabolism is related to the clearance pathway and bioavailability of the specific agent. For example, simvastatin undergoes extensive first-line metabolism by CYP3A4, so its mean oral bioavailability is about 5%; inhibition of CYP3A4 could lead to significant increases—up to 10-fold—in its bioavailability. Conversely, atorvastatin has a higher mean oral bioavailability of 15% to 30%, so concentrations are only modestly increased by CYP3A inhibitors. Another consideration is how the grapefruit or juice does it take to have a significant impact on CYP3A? Several small pharmacokinetic studies suggest that it takes more than 500 mL of grapefruit juice on a daily basis to affect drug metabolism. Consumption of 600 to 750 mL of grapefruit juice each day has been shown to increase the plasma concentration of atorvastatin by 1.3-fold. However, it is quite rare to consume that much grapefruit or grapefruit juice on a regular basis. One study evaluated more commonly consumed amounts: one 300-mL (10-oz) glass of grapefruit juice per day for 90 days in long-term atorvastatin users. Participants were randomly assigned to continue their usual atorvastatin dosage or to decrease their baseline dosage by 50% when starting the grapefruit juice. Participants who continued their baseline dosage had an increase in atorvastatin plasma concentrations of up to 26% compared with concentrations at baseline with negligible changes in lipid levels and no adverse effects on liver function or serum creatine phosphokinase values. Participants randomly assigned to decrease their baseline dosage by 50% had a decrease in plasma atorvastatin concentrations of 12% to 25%, with an increase in LDL-cholesterol concentration (9%) and triglycerides (23%) compared with baseline concentrations. Again, no adverse effects on liver function or creatine phosphokinase levels were observed. Thus, on the basis of this study's findings, the Answer E is correct—no change in statin dosage is needed. The prescribing information raises concern with “excessive grapefruit juice consumption >1.2 liters/day.” If this patient had any other risk factors such as use of other C. some calcium channel blockers, some macrolide antibiotics), were frail, or were taking multiple medications, then decreasing the statin dosage (Answer B) might be reasonable. Given this patient's high risk for a recurrent cardiovascular event, discontinuing the statin, even if starting an alternative agent such as a fibrate or ezetimibe, would not be appropriate (thus, Answers A, C, D are incorrect). It is important to note that different statins are subject to different metabolism, and thus the response to grapefruit juice or other inhibitors of CYP3A4 is agent specific. Most studies evaluating the effect of grapefruit� statins are relatively short term (see Table).
Table. Effects of Grapefruit on Statin Metabolism.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 52: "Counsel a patient on the pharmacology of statins and diet-drug interactions, specifically the effect of grapefruit and grapefruit juice."

items.title: Why answer E is correct
items.text: Continue the current statin at the same dosage
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 52: "Counsel a patient on the pharmacology of statins and diet-drug interactions, specifically the effect of grapefruit and grapefruit juice."
